segments.text: Effector Responses
Dendritic cells phagocytose antigen, display on MHX II, then migrate to secondary organs and display to Helper T-cells

Cellular interactions involved in induction of immune responses
Activation and proliferation of TH cells (a) is required for generation of humoral  response (b) and
cell mediated response to altered self-cells (c).

Antibody Effector Functions

Inflammation
Complex, interacting set of triggers
Contact with pathogens, injury, up-regulate the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (Il-1, Tnfa, IL-6) at sites of developing inflammation

Inflammation
Swelling (tumor), redness (rubor), heat (calor), pain (dolor)

Pamps
Tnfa
Extravasation
Movement of blood cells through an unruptured blood vessel wall into the surrounding tissue, particularly at sites of inflammation

Endothelial cells secrete a diverse range of chemoattractants including:
-IL-8
-Monocyte chemotactic proteins 1 and 3 (MCP-1 and MCP-3)
-regulated upon activation, normal T cell expressed and secreted (RANTES)
-eotaxin
-Platelet-Activating –Factor (PAF)

Chemokines
chemotactic cytokines.

Interleukin-8 (IL-8)
-chemokine
-synthesized by many cell types including endothelial cells, macrophages
-primary target is neutrophils.
-IL-8 has different effects at different concentrations, low stimulates chemoattraction, high concentrations-stimulate degranulation

Activated T lymphocytes have the greatest variety of chemokine receptors
CCR5 is HIV co-receptor

In early stages of inflammatory response, the predominant cell type infiltrating the tissue is the neutrophil.
Neutrophil infiltrations peaks within the first 6 hours

Neutrophils
-also called polymorphonuclear (PMNs) leukocytes
-short lifespan 6-8 hours in circulation
-terminally differentiated
-immortalized cells not very similar to natural cells
-mice neutrophils are pretty different than humans
-release from bone marrow is tightly regulated, chemokines cause it
-its “antimicrobial weapons are deadly, indiscriminate, and brutish.  Can be just as dangerous to the host.”

Non-oxidative neutrophil proteins:
Bactericidal/Permeability Increasing 	(BPI) protein
Proteases
lysozyme
defensins
cathelicidins

Toxic compounds generated in neutrophils to destroy ingested micro-organisms (neutrophils, macrophages, dendritic cells)
Bleach
Neutrophil Extracellular Traps (NETs)

-Mainly use phagocytosis
-Display toll-like receptors to detect bacteria
-Also recognize Complement proteins or antibodies on opsonized antigens
Neutrophils
NETS
Macrophages
Macrophage are only second to the liver in Complement Synthesis
Antibody dependent cell mediated cytotoxicity

Cytotoxic T cells
CD8+ Recognize MHC I- viral infected or tumor cells

Naïve TC cell differentiation into CTL
Dendritic antigen presenting cell
CTL

CTLs kill cells
Chemical assault with Perforin and serine proteases (granzymes)
Activation of Apoptosis
Perforin

Granzyme
One of a set of enzymes (serine proteases) found in the granules of Tc cells that can help initiate apoptosis in target cells

Target cell
CTL
CTL killing of target cell
Summary of ways that Cytotoxic T-Cells can kill other cells

CTL killing
Apoptosis/Perforin, Granzyme

Natural Killer cells (NK) are related to CTL’s and kill in much the same way
Also kill cells lacking MHC surface molecules

Summary of cell killing
Neutrophils
-Phagocytosis
-Reactive oxygen synthesis
-Anti-microbial proteins
-NETS
Macrophages
-See neutrophils
-Secrete complement
CTL
-Perforins, granzymes
-Induced apoptosis
Natural Killer Cells
-see CTL

Important Terms/Concepts
Mast cell characteristics, activation and response
Activities of histamine, TNFa
Endothelial cells, Il-8
Neutrophil killing ability: Phagocytosis, Reactive Oxygen 	and Nitrogen Species, Anti-microbial proteins
Macrophage-similar to Neutrophils, with additional 	complement synthesis ability
Cytotoxic T lymphocytes
CTL killing mechanism: Perforin, granzymes, apoptosis
Natural Killer Cells: similar to CTL
Perforin pores on target cell

The major pro-inflammatory cytokines that are responsible for early responses are IL-1a, IL-6 TNFa.
Other pro-inflammatory mediators include, IFNg, TGF-b, GM-CSF, IL-11, IL-12, IL-17, IL-8
Blood cells on average spend about 1 minute in the systemic circulation for a round trip

Naive T-cells differentiate after antigen presentation
Naïve CD4+ or TH cells differentiate into:
TH1-activation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes, 			 	 helper for B-cell activation, hypersensitivity
TH2-very effective helper for B-cell activation
Naïve CD8+, or TC cells differentiate into
Cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs)

Cytokines direct naïve CD4+ cells into either a TH1 or TH2
-Promotes TC growth
-activates macrophages
-Hypersensitivity
-Activates B-cell production of antibodies
-very effectively activates B-cell production of antibodies

Lymphocytes bound to HEVs
High Endothelial Venules (HEVs) are sites of lymphocyte extravasation (lymph node)

Mast cells
-key players in the initiation of inflammation
-are found in all vascularized tissues.
-Their cytoplasm is loaded with granules containing mediators of inflammation.
-Their surface is coated with a variety of receptors which, when engaged by the appropriate ligand, trigger exocytosis of the granules.
-Toll-like receptors trigger exocytosis when they interact with Pathogen Associated Molecular Patterns (PAMPs) like
The lipopolysaccharide  LPS or “endotoxin” of Gram negative bacteria (TLR-4)
The peptidoglycan of Gram-positive bacteria (TLR-2)
-Their receptors for complement fragments trigger exocytosis when they bind C3a and C5a or bacteria coated with C3d

Mast Cells release:
TNFa-pro-inflammatory cytokine
Histamine-increases permeability of capillaries for leukocytes

Extravasation
Movement of blood cells through an unruptured blood vessel wall into the surrounding tissue, particularly at sites of inflammation

Interleukin-8 (IL-8)
-chemokine
-synthesized by many cell types including endothelial cells, macrophages
-primary target is neutrophils.
IL-8 has different effects at different concentrations.
High concentrations-stimulate degranulation

Eosinophils
Can have minor phagocytotic activity
Secrete lytic enzymes, perforin, and some neurotoxins
